content.problemStatement:
There were NNN people lined up in a queue. The people are numbered 111, 222, …\ldots…, NNN but not necessarily in the order they are lined up.

Unfortunately, some of them can't even understand where the front of the queue is. So, some people are looking towards the front of the queue and some people are looking towards the back of the queue.

The iii-th person reports AiA_iAi​ as the number of people ahead of him **in the direction** he is looking.

Now, you are wondering how many possible orders of people could be there in the queue. 222 orders are said to be different if at least 111 position iii, such that the iii-th person from the front of the queue are different.

Since the answer may be large, output it modulo 998244353998244353998244353. The answer may also be 000, i.e. no valid order exists.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line contains NNN - the number of people in the queue.
The second line contains NNN integers - A1,A2,...,ANA_1, A_2, ..., A_NA1​,A2​,...,AN​.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the number of possible orders modulo 998244353998244353998244353.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 2⋅1052 \cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1 : Both [1,2][1, 2][1,2] and [2,1][2, 1][2,1] are valid orders. For example, in the order [1,2][1, 2][1,2] it is possible the first person looks towards the front, and the second person looks towards the back. This way, both will count 000 people ahead of them in the direction they are facing.
Test Case 2 : [1,2,3][1, 2, 3][1,2,3] and [3,2,1][3, 2, 1][3,2,1] are possible.
Test Case 3 : No valid order exists.
